book_summary: 1 Samuel chapters 1-24 are complete in restored/source-audited draft and mystical companion layers. The book now moves from Hannah, Samuel, Shiloh's abusive priesthood, ark capture and return, the royal-extraction warning, Saul's rise, destructive oaths, the Amalek genocide text, and David's anointing into David and Goliath, Jonathan's covenant, Saul's spear attacks and dynastic fear, Merab and Michal under royal marriage strategy, David's flight, Michal's rescue, sanctuary at Ramah, Jonathan's coded warning and farewell, holy bread and Goliath's sword, David's fear at Gath, the Adullam refugee community, Moabite refuge, the state massacre of Nob, Abiathar's survival, Keilah, surveillance and wilderness pursuit, Jonathan's final recorded meeting, and David's refusal to assassinate Saul while maintaining distance. Safeguards reject child-soldier romanticization, body shaming, bullying, ethnic and religious slurs, weapon glorification, beheading and trophy violence, queer erasure, misogynistic blame, domestic and workplace violence, coerced marriage, genital mutilation as courtship, mental-health stigma, refugee contempt, clerical massacre apologetics, unlawful-order obedience, siege and surveillance theology, leader cults, 'touch not the anointed' immunity, compulsory forgiveness, and forced reconciliation.

translation_notes: Fertility, child dedication, clerical authority, disability, illness, prophecy, war, monarchy, and anointing throughout chapters 1-16 retain the established safeguards against shame, coercion, abuse, stigma, genocide, and leader immunity.
David's age in chapter 17 is not specified beyond youth language. The narrative may not recruit minors into danger or teach that courage requires child participation in adult warfare.
Goliath's height and the chapter's length differ across textual witnesses. Body size is not moral symbolism, and the source seams must remain visible.
The David-and-Goliath account includes royal incentives, lethal weapons, beheading, corpse exposure, pursuit, casualties, and plunder; it is not harmless children's spectacle or a holy-war template.
Jonathan and David's relationship is reciprocal, intimate, political, and intergenerational. Queer readings may not be erased, and no single modern identity label may be coercively imposed.
The women's song does not cause Saul's jealousy or violence. Women are not responsible for men's rivalry or abuse.
Saul's harmful-spirit language cannot diagnose mental illness, trauma, neurodivergence, disability, seizures, or distress. Saul remains responsible for spear attacks, surveillance, and murder orders.
Merab and Michal are not royal property, rewards, or natural snares. Their consent is not established by their father's arrangements.
The foreskin bride-price is ethnicized killing, genital mutilation, and trophy violence; it cannot be celebrated, eroticized, or taught as masculinity.
Jonathan's and Michal's resistance shows that family and political loyalty do not require concealing threats or obeying an abusive ruler.
Saul's broken oath and later tears do not by themselves establish safety. Survivors may hide, flee, deceive under threat, seek sanctuary, and maintain distance.
Prophetic altered states, disrobing, saliva, drooling, and performed disorientation may not be mocked or used to stigmatize mental illness or disability.
Ahimelech's hospitality does not make him responsible for Saul's massacre. David's deception and later regret require accountability without transferring perpetrator guilt.
Nob is a state massacre of priests, residents, children, nursing infants, and animals. The servants' refusal demonstrates the moral legitimacy of resisting unlawful orders.
Doeg's Edomite identity does not explain his violence; ethnicity is never inherited guilt.
People in debt, distress, bitterness, exile, or refugee communities retain dignity and are not presumptively criminal.
Saul repeatedly invokes YHWH while planning siege, surveillance, and persecution. Religious certainty is not moral proof.
Keilah's likely surrender under pressure and the Ziphites' cooperation may not justify hatred of whole cities, regions, or peoples.
'Anointed of YHWH' cannot shield rulers, clergy, or charismatic leaders from investigation, removal, restraint, or justice.
David's refusal to kill Saul rejects assassination but does not require survivors to reconcile, restore trust, remove boundaries, or return to unsafe proximity.
Mystical companion layers remain interpretive and never replace restored/source-audited text.
source_witness_audit_flags: 1 Samuel 1-16 retain their existing philological, textual-witness, medical-history, genocide, mental-health, child-safeguarding, interfaith, and political-theology audit flags.

1 Samuel 17 MT/Greek length differences, David's prior court relation, Goliath height variants, armor weights, reward traditions, age language, lion/bear claims, forehead reading, death sequence, Jerusalem/tent chronology, and Saul's lineage questions require maximum source and ethical audit.

1 Samuel 18 Jonathan's nephesh-binding and love vocabulary, robe/weapon transfer, women's song, harmful-spirit/raving terms, spear attempts, Merab transfer, Michal's love, double son-in-law wording, bride-price count, genital trophy, and Greek omissions require textual, queer-reception, gender, and violence audit.

1 Samuel 19 murder-order chronology, Jonathan's oath intervention, harmful-spirit recurrence, Michal's teraphim and goat-hair object, coercive explanation, Naioth terminology, prophetic-company practice, messenger alteration, Saul's disrobing, and proverb require witness, sanctuary, and mental-health audit.

1 Samuel 20 new-moon meal practice, purity explanation, covenant formulas, hesed toward descendants, arrow code, Ezel, Saul's misogynistic insult, death-language, child messenger, kiss/weeping wording, and final verse division require philological, queer-reception, and safeguarding audit.

1 Samuel 21 Nob location, David's deception, young-men location, holy-bread purity logic, Doeg's detention and office, Goliath sword tradition, Achish identity, altered-behavior verbs, gate marks, saliva, and chapter-number relation to Hebrew tradition require witness and disability audit.

1 Samuel 22 Adullam community vocabulary, Moab refuge, Gad tradition, Saul's patronage speech, Doeg's office and ethnicity, inquiry wording, priest number, Nob massacre scope, Abiathar genealogy, and David's guilt statement require maximum textual, massacre, refugee, and command-responsibility audit.

1 Samuel 23 Keilah geography and threshing floors, inquiry sequence, livestock seizure, ephod use, Saul's 'closed him' theology, conditional oracle, troop number, Jonathan succession language, Ziphite motives, surveillance terms, Maon geography, and Rock of Escape name require source and political-theology audit.

1 Samuel 24 chapter numbering, En-gedi geography, 'cover feet' euphemism, robe-edge royal symbolism, conscience verb, vayeshassa restraint term, anointed rhetoric, dead-dog/flea speech, Saul's confession, succession acknowledgment, descendant oath, and separate departures require philological, abuse, and reception-history audit.
next_actions: Complete 1 Samuel with chapters 25-31: Samuel's death, Nabal and Abigail, David's threatened household massacre, Abigail's intervention and marriages, David sparing Saul at Hachilah, Gath and Ziklag refuge, raids and deception, Philistine mobilization, the medium of Endor and Samuel apparition tradition, Saul's terror, Philistine rejection of David, Ziklag's destruction and captive recovery, the abandoned Egyptian servant, spoil-sharing law, Saul and Jonathan's deaths, self-inflicted death and assisted-killing tensions, body desecration at Beth-shan, and Jabesh-gilead burial—with maximum anti-misogyny, anti-coercive-marriage, anti-household-massacre, anti-medium-persecution, anti-occult-panic, anti-slavery, anti-suicide-misuse, anti-body-desecration, anti-ethnic-hatred, and anti-leader-cult safeguards.
